element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Player must understand food, production, and commerce mechanics from turn 1
Incremental - irrigation increases food, mines increase production, roads increase commerce

element_introduction_order.first_appears: early game after initial tech unlocks
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Require prerequisite technologies and ongoing maintenance; only one of each type per city
Unique empire-wide benefits; race against other civilizations for construction
Can be built multiple times per civilization; sociological + technological requirements
Divided into four ages (Ancient, Middle, Industrial, Modern); unlocks units, buildings, wonders, and special abilities

element_introduction_order.first_appears: early game after minimal tech investment
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Requires technologies; trained to engage in warfare with neighboring civilizations
Right of passage treaties, technology brokering, gold/resource exchanges with other civilizations

element_introduction_order.element: City citizen management (Laborers, Entertainers, Tax Collectors, Scientists)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Citizens automatically become Entertainers if more citizens than available tiles; affects production and happiness
Unhappy citizens cause civil disorder and production cessation; happy citizens increase economic benefits

element_introduction_order.first_appears: early game (new feature in Civ III)
element_introduction_order.mastery_context: Each city has cultural influence; mechanics not fully detailed in source but present from start
Policemen reduce corruption; Civil Engineers enhance building and wonder production

typical_relief_triggers: Completing technology research unlocks new units/buildings/wonders
Successfully constructing a Wonder or Small Wonder
Establishing peace treaties with aggressive neighbors
Moving to a new age (Ancient → Middle → Industrial → Modern)
typical_tension_triggers: City falling into civil disorder due to unhappy citizens
Race to build limited Wonders before competitors
Resource scarcity or poor tile management
rhythm_pattern.pacing_descriptor: Turn-based progression allowing player-controlled pacing; tension builds through competition for wonders and diplomatic/military threats; relief through technology breakthroughs and successful city management

spatial_grammar.optional_content_signal: Tile improvements and terrain types signal resource potential; unexplored map areas create expansion opportunities

difficulty_curve.early_game: Learning curve: establishing first cities, understanding terrain/tile mechanics, balancing food/production/commerce
difficulty_curve.mid_game: Managing multiple cities, competing for Wonders, researching through Middle Age technologies, handling diplomatic relationships
difficulty_curve.late_game: Large empire management, cultural influence, advancing through Industrial and Modern ages, potential military dominance or diplomatic/cultural victories
notable_spikes: Transition between ages (Ancient → Middle → Industrial → Modern) requiring new strategic approaches
Wonder construction races creating high-stakes competition windows
Military threats from neighboring civilizations escalating with better units

notable_level_patterns.description: Game divided into four distinct ages; advancement requires completing specific technology research chains; each age unlocks new strategic options
Unique Wonders and Small Wonders create periodic high-tension competition phases where civilizations race to construct benefits before opponents
Cyclic tension/relief pattern where cities can fall into civil disorder if citizens are unhappy, destroying improvements and halting production until resolved
Difficulty and complexity increase as player expands from early single city to large multi-city empires requiring coordinated management of resources, happiness, and maintenance costs
